items.title: Use the built-ins
items.avoid: Hand-rolling a hash table for production code.
items.why: CPython's implementations are highly tuned and battle-tested.
items.example:
```python
from collections import Counter
freq = Counter(tokens)
```

items.title: Pre-size for bulk loads
items.avoid: Inserting a million items one at a time into an empty dict and hoping for the best.
items.prefer: Use the dict comprehension / constructor with the full input so CPython sizes the table appropriately.

items.title: Make custom keys hashable correctly
items.prefer: Use `@dataclass(frozen=True)` or define both `__eq__` and `__hash__` consistently.
items.why: Equal objects must produce equal hashes; otherwise dict/set lookups silently fail.
items.example:
```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class DocRef:
    id: str
    rev: int
```

items.title: Use a real cryptographic hash for stable cross-run keys
items.avoid: Storing `hash(text)` as a long-lived cache key on disk.
items.why: Python's `hash` is randomized per process; SHA-256 is stable and collision-resistant.
